scenarios.task: I'm charge nurse getting a verbal update from a primary RN on 4 West. Patient is POD2 total hip, was NEWS2 of 0 at 0700. At 0900 she's HR 102, RR 22, SpO2 94% RA, BP 118/70, temp 37.8, still alert, but mentioned feeling a little short of breath walking to the chair. The primary nurse says the score comes out to a 4, which is still 'low risk,' and wants to know if she should just finish the rest of her med pass first or come back to this patient sooner.
criteria: Explicitly reframes the 0900 reading as a trend (0 to 4 in two hours) rather than judging the single aggregate score in isolation
States that the new subjective dyspnea is itself meaningful clinical data, not something to wait out until the monitor confirms it
Recommends increasing reassessment frequency to roughly 30 minutes rather than the standard interval, and deferring the rest of the med-pass round rather than finishing it first
Names the specific post-op risk this trend points toward (e.g. PE/DVT given recent hip surgery and reduced mobility) as the reason not to dismiss it
Does not recommend escalating to physician/RRT yet at this stage, correctly distinguishing 'increase monitoring' from 'escalate now' as separate lanes

scenarios.task: Same patient, now at 1015: HR 118, RR 26, BP 96/58, SpO2 91% on room air (93% on 2L NC), temp 38.3, and she's now only oriented to person. I'm the nurse and I need to know exactly what to do right now and how to actually get the on-call hospitalist to move fast instead of getting queued behind three other pages.
criteria: Calculates or cites the NEWS2 aggregate (14) and explicitly flags that RR, SpO2, and level of consciousness each independently score the maximum on their own, meeting urgent-review criteria even without the aggregate

Builds a full SBAR (situation, background, assessment, recommendation) rather than a vague 'come take a look,' and the recommendation is a specific, time-bound ask (e.g. bedside within 10 minutes or RRT)

Names both differential concerns the vitals point toward — pulmonary embolism (given recent THA/immobility) and sepsis (fever + tachycardia + confusion) — as data to hand the physician rather than picking one
States that interventions already started (O2 applied, more frequent vitals, IV access) should be reported as part of the SBAR
Specifies documenting the exact time of the call/escalation in the chart

scenarios.task: I'm an RN starting a 1:6 med-surg shift. Handoff report says all six patients are 'stable, uneventful night,' but I've got a new post-op admission, two patients due for IV antibiotics within the hour, and three routine, low-acuity patients. I want to just start at room 1 and work down the hall in order so I don't lose track — does that make sense?
criteria: Rejects room-number/med-pass order as the triage basis, naming that it's an administrative convenience rather than a clinical prioritization method
Recommends triaging all patients into tiers within the first 15-20 minutes using acuity/instability and time since last assessment, not the handoff's 'stable' label alone
States that the new post-op patient should be seen first regardless of room order, and the IV-antibiotic patients should be seen before their scheduled time rather than after
Explicitly says to establish an independent personal baseline on each patient rather than trusting the 'stable, uneventful' handoff at face value
Notes the plan should be re-ranked if any patient's status changes mid-shift, rather than treating the initial order as fixed

scenarios.task: I'm the RN on a busy 1:6 shift. My CNA just told me Patient B's fingerstick glucose came back 'normal' and vitals look fine, but when I glanced in the room a minute ago the patient looked more lethargic than an hour ago and is harder to rouse. I've got two other patients waiting on meds right now — can I just trust the CNA's numbers and deal with this patient after I finish the med pass?
criteria: Says no — a delegated reading reported normal while the patient looks clinically worse must be personally reassessed/reverified before acting on it or moving on, not treated as closing the loop
Cites new lethargy/decreased responsiveness in a previously stable patient as itself an urgent trigger requiring reassessment now, out of sequence, ahead of the med pass
Names specific things to check given new altered responsiveness — repeat glucose, oxygen saturation, and recent opioid/sedative dose and timing — as the first data to pull
Frames delegation as transferring the task but not the accountability for interpreting the result, so 'CNA said normal' doesn't discharge the RN's responsibility
Recommends deferring the two waiting medications (or getting help via charge nurse) rather than finishing the med pass before addressing the lethargic patient
